region.name: Nelson / Tasman
region.description: The sunniest region in the country, stretching from Abel Tasman golden beaches to Farewell Spit and the alpine Nelson Lakes.

pois.name: Abel Tasman National Park
pois.short: NZ's smallest national park and arguably its prettiest — 60 km of gold-sand beaches, turquoise water, and one of the 10 Great Walks.
pois.commentary: Abel Tasman National Park is 237 km² — NZ's smallest — but produces some of the country's most photographed coastline: golden sand, clear water, native bush right to the tideline. The 60 km Abel Tasman Coast Track (one of the 10 Great Walks) runs the full length, with water taxis letting you walk any section you want. Sea kayaking is the other way in — day tours or multi-day paddles through the sheltered bays. Tonga Island Marine Reserve has a resident NZ fur-seal colony. The park takes its name from Dutch navigator Abel Tasman, the first European to sight New Zealand in 1642.

reviews.summary: Abel Tasman National Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. NZ's smallest national park and arguably its prettiest — 60 km of gold-sand beaches, turquoise water, and one of the 10 Great Walks. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Nelson City / Whakatū
pois.short: The country's sunniest city — 2,400+ hours of sunshine a year, an arts scene to match, and the geographic centre of NZ.
pois.commentary: Nelson — Whakatū — has been settled for ~800 years (one of the earliest Māori settlements in Te Waipounamu) and founded as a European town in 1842. It's consistently New Zealand's sunniest city, with 2,400+ hours of sunshine annually. Christ Church Cathedral on its hill (completed 1972 after 47 years of construction) anchors the skyline. The World of WearableArt Museum, Founders Heritage Park, and the Suter Gallery are cultural draws. And the exact geographic centre of New Zealand sits on a hill 10 minutes' walk from the cathedral — a short track with a signpost at the top.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Nelson City / Whakatū is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The country's sunniest city — 2,400+ hours of sunshine a year, an arts scene to match, and the geographic centre of NZ. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Nelson Lakes / Lake Rotoiti / St Arnaud
pois.short: The alpine front door to the Nelson Lakes National Park — glacial lakes, kea, and a DOC-led predator-control sanctuary.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotoiti sits at St Arnaud, the gateway village to Nelson Lakes National Park. The lake was carved by a glacier during the last Ice Age; the bush-clad ranges behind rise to 2,500 m. Nelson Lakes is one of DOC's 'Mainland Island' intensive predator-control zones, and native birdlife has returned strongly — kea at the ski field, bellbirds in the bush, even rarer species recolonising. Short walks include the iconic jetty-at-dawn photo; longer options include the Mount Robert circuit and the 7-day Travers-Sabine circuit.

reviews.summary: Nelson Lakes / Lake Rotoiti / St Arnaud draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The alpine front door to the Nelson Lakes National Park — glacial lakes, kea, and a DOC-led predator-control sanctuary. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Farewell Spit / Tuhuroa
pois.short: A 26 km sand spit — New Zealand's longest — and a Ramsar-listed bird sanctuary at the top of the South Island.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit is 26 km of sand and shell — the longest natural sand spit in New Zealand, growing roughly 10 m a year from the sediment the Westland rivers pour into Cook Strait. It is an internationally significant Ramsar wetland: up to 20,000 bar-tailed godwits arrive here every September from Alaska, completing the world's longest non-stop bird migration (~11,000 km). Public access is restricted; DOC permits the first 4 km, and only licensed tour operators (two, from Collingwood) go further. The 1870 lighthouse at the tip is their turnaround point.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Farewell Spit / Tuhuroa — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A 26 km sand spit — New Zealand's longest — and a Ramsar-listed bird sanctuary at the top of the South Island. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Golden Bay / Mohua / Tākaka
pois.short: Over Tākaka Hill — a self-sufficient, idiosyncratic community the rest of the country politely calls 'alternative'.
pois.commentary: Golden Bay — Mohua — is the curve of coastline north of the Tākaka Hill and culturally somewhat separate from the rest of the country. From the 1970s it attracted back-to-the-land settlers, permaculture pioneers, and dropouts; the resulting community is artisan-heavy, organic, friendly, and genuinely different. Tākaka is the main town: a single main street of cafés, galleries, a legendary bakery, and the Dangerous Kitchen. Pōhara and Tata Beach are the swimming beaches. Pupu Springs, Wharariki, the Abel Tasman, and Farewell Spit are all within 45 minutes. Summer only — the hill road gets icy in winter.

reviews.summary: Golden Bay / Mohua / Tākaka draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Over Tākaka Hill — a self-sufficient, idiosyncratic community the rest of the country politely calls 'alternative'. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Wharariki Beach
pois.short: The Archway Islands — four sea arches in the sand at low tide. Windows 10 set its opening wallpaper here.
pois.commentary: Wharariki Beach is 20 minutes of walking from the nearest road, over a farm, past a dune — and when you arrive you face one of the great wild beaches of New Zealand. The Archway Islands, four volcanic sea stacks with huge arches, stand in the surf 100 m offshore. At low tide they appear to sit on the sand; Microsoft used them as the default Windows 10 opening screensaver. A small fur-seal colony often visits. The beach faces straight into the Tasman and the surf is dangerous. Take a jumper even in summer; the wind is constant.

reviews.summary: Wharariki Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The Archway Islands — four sea arches in the sand at low tide. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Te Waikoropupū Springs / Pupu Springs
pois.short: The clearest freshwater ever measured — and a wāhi tapu. No touching, no swimming, just looking.
pois.commentary: Te Waikoropupū Springs pump out 14 cubic metres of water per second from an underground aquifer, making them one of the largest freshwater springs in the Southern Hemisphere. In 1993, NIWA measured the water's visibility at 63 metres — a global record for freshwater clarity (only pure distilled water exceeds it). The springs are a wāhi tapu to Ngāti Tama and Te Ātiawa; after decades of concern over degradation and unauthorised swimming, a 2020 agreement strictly prohibits contact with the water. A boardwalk loops through the springs — walk, look, leave.
sources_to_check: Ngāti Tama ki Te Tau Ihu

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs / Pupu Springs as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The clearest freshwater ever measured — and a wāhi tapu. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri Beach
pois.short: The gateway beach to Abel Tasman — gold sand, calm water, and the family beach of choice for the top of the South Island.
pois.commentary: Kaiteriteri is the small beach village on the southern edge of Abel Tasman National Park. The beach is a perfect crescent of gold sand (fine granite weathered out of the inland ranges), sheltered water, and year-round warm swimming. It's the embarkation point for many Abel Tasman water taxis and kayak tours. The hinterland has a small, well-made mountain-bike park — Kaiteriteri Mountain Bike Park, free entry, grades 2–5. The holiday park fills in summer; book far ahead.

reviews.summary: Kaiteriteri Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The gateway beach to Abel Tasman — gold sand, calm water, and the family beach of choice for the top of the South Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Mapua Wharf
pois.short: A pretty little wharf village between Nelson and Motueka — good food, a small ferry to Rabbit Island, and relaxed afternoon energy.
pois.commentary: Mapua sits on the Waimea Estuary, 35 minutes west of Nelson. The wharf — once an apple-export loading dock — has been revived as a cluster of restaurants, craft breweries, fish-and-chip shop, and artisan studios. The Mapua Ferry runs a 5-minute crossing to Rabbit Island (Moturoa) for swimming, picnicking and walking. It's a good afternoon stop on the drive to Motueka or Abel Tasman — busy in summer, quiet shoulder seasons, and one of the nicest-looking public spaces in the Nelson region.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Mapua Wharf is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A pretty little wharf village between Nelson and Motueka — good food, a small ferry to Rabbit Island, and relaxed afternoon energy. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: World of WearableArt (WOW)
pois.short: Wearable art competition pieces and a classic car collection, displayed together — only in Nelson.
pois.commentary: WearableArt began in Nelson in 1987 as a local gallery promotion — 'art off the wall, onto the body' — and grew into a major annual international competition (now held in Wellington). The museum in Nelson displays rotating selections of the most striking past entries: sculptural fashion made from paua, broken glass, car parts, fish skins, computer components. In the same building and with the same ticket, an enthusiast-sourced collection of classic cars, from a 1908 Cadillac to a De Lorean. An unexpected pairing; both parts excellent.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at World of WearableArt (WOW) is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Wearable art competition pieces and a classic car collection, displayed together — only in Nelson. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Centre of New Zealand Monument
pois.short: A short steep walk to a survey trig that has become Nelson's symbolic geographic centre of the country.
pois.commentary: The monument marks the point from which John Spence Browning triangulated the South Island in the 1870s, not the true geographic centre (which lies further south).
pois.audio_story: You are walking up to the Centre of New Zealand monument, a short but steep climb from the Botanical Reserve in central Nelson. The story of the name is more interesting than the geography. In the eighteen seventies, the colonial surveyor John Spence Browning established a network of triangulation points across the South Island, and one of his key stations was on top of this hill. Over the years the story morphed, as stories do, and the hill came to be described as the exact geographic centre of New Zealand. In truth, it is not. The true centre lies further south, in the Spooners Range above Tapawera. But the name stuck, and the view from the top is so generous that no one has really minded. The walk up is fifteen minutes of steady climbing on a well formed path. At the top you stand on a small flat summit with a trig, a flagpole, and a ring of interpretation boards. On a clear day the whole of Nelson is spread below you, with Tasman Bay glinting north, the Richmond Range to the east, and the Western Ranges of Abel Tasman National Park on the horizon. It is one of the great quick walks of any New Zealand town.

reviews.summary: Centre of New Zealand Monument draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A short steep walk to a survey trig that has become Nelson's symbolic geographic centre of the country. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Heaphy Track
pois.short: The longest of the Great Walks — 82km through Kahurangi National Park, from the Aorere Valley to the West Coast.
pois.commentary: Four-to-five day tramp, or 2-3 day mountain bike ride in winter. One of only two Great Walks open to cyclists.
pois.audio_story: You are about to walk the Heaphy Track, the longest of the ten Great Walks of New Zealand. Eighty two kilometres on foot, usually over four or five days, from the Aorere Valley in the Tasman region across the spine of Kahurangi National Park down to the wild west coast at Kohaihai, north of Karamea. The route crosses an astonishing range of landscapes. It begins in farmland, climbs into deep beech forest, breaks out onto the high Gouland Downs of tussock and limestone karst, then descends through nikau palm forest to emerge, finally, on a long wild beach on the Tasman Sea. In winter the track is also one of only two Great Walks open to mountain bikers, and the two or three day ride across it is one of the most sought after mountain biking experiences in the country. Kahurangi holds many of the oldest rocks and strangest endemic species in New Zealand, including the large flightless great spotted kiwi, which you may well hear at night from one of the well appointed huts along the route. Book huts well in advance through Department of Conservation. Allow a good weather window. The Heaphy, in fine weather, is one of the great walks of your life.

reviews.summary: Heaphy Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The longest of the Great Walks — 82km through Kahurangi National Park, from the Aorere Valley to the West Coast. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Motueka
pois.short: The laid-back gateway to Abel Tasman — a Saturday market, hop farms, and more wholefood cafes than traffic lights.
pois.commentary: Motueka is the service town for Abel Tasman National Park. Known for hops, apples, and a thriving arts/alternative community.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Motueka, the gentle service town at the southern entrance to Abel Tasman National Park, and you have arrived somewhere slightly unlike the rest of New Zealand. Motueka sits in a small sheltered plain between the mountains and the sea, with a climate mild enough to grow world class hops, apples, and boysenberries, and a human community to match. The town has long attracted alternative makers, artists, organic farmers and slow living migrants, and the main street today reflects that mix, with a disproportionate number of wholefood cafes, art galleries, and bookshops for a town of seven and a half thousand people. On Sunday mornings the Motueka Market spreads through the town centre with craft stalls, local produce, and some of the best cheap food in the region. From Motueka you can drive fifteen minutes to Kaiteriteri and the start of Abel Tasman water taxis, or ten minutes to the Riwaka Resurgence, a turquoise pool where the Riwaka River emerges fully grown from a limestone cave. Many visitors treat Motueka only as a passing stop. Stay a night. Catch the market. Linger a little.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Motueka is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The laid-back gateway to Abel Tasman — a Saturday market, hop farms, and more wholefood cafes than traffic lights. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Rabbit Island / Moturoa
pois.short: A huge pine-shaded beach on a flat recreational island across the causeway from Nelson.
pois.commentary: Moturoa/Rabbit Island is a flat 12km-long island connected by causeway. Dedicated entirely to recreation — pine-shaded camping, 8km-long safe swimming beach.
pois.audio_story: You have driven out across the causeway to Moturoa, known universally in Nelson as Rabbit Island, and you have found yourself on an island entirely dedicated to recreation. Twelve kilometres of flat land, the whole of it planted in a mature pine forest on one side and a wide safe swimming beach on the other. In summer the island fills up with Nelson families spending the day here. There are picnic tables scattered under the pines, kilometres of flat cycling and walking tracks through the forest, and a long gentle beach where the water is sheltered by the Arthur Range on the far side of the bay and warms to unusually pleasant temperatures by mid-summer. Mountain bikers have a network of purpose built single track lines through the pines, family riders can follow the flat sealed and gravel roads, and walkers can loop the shoreline. A small cable ferry runs across the narrow Waimea Inlet to Mapua village, where cafes, breweries and fish and chips are waiting. The island is a working forestry plantation, so some roads are closed during active logging, but the great majority of it is open year round. Free entry. Free parking. Bring a picnic and a bike.

reviews.summary: Rabbit Island / Moturoa draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A huge pine-shaded beach on a flat recreational island across the causeway from Nelson. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Boulder Bank & Nelson Haven
pois.short: The natural 13km boulder spit that created Nelson''s safe harbour — and made settlement possible in 1841.
pois.commentary: Boulder Bank is a unique 13km natural seawall that creates Nelson Haven. The NZ Company founded Nelson 1841 inside this shelter.
pois.audio_story: You are looking at the Boulder Bank, a single thirteen kilometre natural shingle and boulder ridge that encloses Nelson Haven. It is an unusual landform, found nowhere else quite like it in New Zealand. The boulders, worked down over centuries from the schist cliffs of Mackay Bluff to the north, have been pushed south by the prevailing currents and deposited in this long narrow spit parallel to the mainland. The result is a sheltered harbour that would not otherwise exist on this exposed stretch of coast. When the New Zealand Company began looking for a second settlement site after Wellington in eighteen forty one, they chose Nelson precisely because the Boulder Bank provided a ready made protected anchorage. The surveyor Arthur Wakefield sailed in through the Haven entrance in late eighteen forty one with the first settler ships. The town was laid out the following year. The Māori name for the feature is Te Matau a Māui, recalling the fish hook of Māui, and the area was already well known to Te Ātiawa, Ngāti Kōata, and Ngāti Rārua who had settled the sheltered side of the bank. A lighthouse built on the outer end in eighteen sixty two still operates. Drive the Boulder Bank road at the northern end if you want to walk on it.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Boulder Bank & Nelson Haven — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The natural 13km boulder spit that created Nelson''s safe harbour — and made settlement possible in 1841. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Nelson Cathedral — Christ Church Cathedral
pois.short: The Anglican cathedral that took nearly 50 years to complete — a curious mix of Gothic planning and art deco detailing.
pois.commentary: Began 1925 on a hill overlooking Nelson. Construction paused during the Depression, WWII, and seismic assessments. Finally consecrated 1972.
pois.audio_story: You are at Christ Church Cathedral, Nelson, which stands on Church Hill overlooking the city centre. The cathedral has an unusually long and broken construction history. Work began in nineteen twenty five on the site of an earlier wooden church that had stood since eighteen fifty one. The original plan called for a soaring neo Gothic building in the tradition of the great English cathedrals. Construction was then paused by the Depression of the nineteen thirties, by the Second World War, and by successive seismic reviews that progressively reduced the ambition of the design. By the time work finally resumed after the war, the original lofty masonry tower plans had been abandoned as too heavy, and a simplified reinforced concrete and Takaka marble tower, drawing on art deco proportions rather than pure gothic, was substituted. The cathedral was finally consecrated in nineteen seventy two, forty seven years after the foundation stone was laid. The result is surprisingly harmonious, a genuine hybrid of twentieth century Modernism with Gothic bones. The steps up Church Hill to the front porch are a Nelson landmark in their own right, forming the visual centrepiece of the long Trafalgar Street axis. Walk up, go inside, and take ten minutes.
sources_to_check: Heritage NZ — Christ Church Cathedral Nelson

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Nelson Cathedral — Christ Church Cathedral — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The Anglican cathedral that took nearly 50 years to complete — a curious mix of Gothic planning and art deco detailing. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Wakefield Quay — Founding Settlers Memorial
pois.short: The quay named for Captain Arthur Wakefield — founder of the Nelson settlement, killed at Wairau the following year.
pois.commentary: Captain Arthur Wakefield was NZ Company resident agent for Nelson from late 1841. Killed with 21 others at Tuamarina in the Wairau Affray, June 1843.
pois.audio_story: You are at Wakefield Quay, the Nelson waterfront named for Captain Arthur Wakefield, the founder of the Nelson settlement. Arthur was the younger brother of Edward Gibbon Wakefield, the controversial theorist behind the systematic colonisation movement, and of William Wakefield, who had led the earlier Wellington settlement. Arthur arrived here in October eighteen forty one aboard the Whitby, along with the survey party sent ahead to prepare for the main body of colonists. Over the next eighteen months he supervised the landing of the first one thousand eight hundred settlers, the laying out of the town grid you see today, the early farming allocations, and the surveying of outlying districts including the lush Wairau Plains to the east. In June eighteen forty three he led a large armed expedition to Wairau to enforce a disputed survey against the Ngāti Toa chiefs Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata. The expedition ended in disaster. Arthur Wakefield and twenty one others were killed in what is now called the Wairau Affray, the first major armed conflict between colonists and Māori after the Treaty. A memorial stone on the quay names him and several of his colleagues. Nelson in its modern form is in many ways Arthur Wakefield''s legacy, both the town that exists and the limits his death placed on early colonial confidence.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Wakefield Quay — Founding Settlers Memorial — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The quay named for Captain Arthur Wakefield — founder of the Nelson settlement, killed at Wairau the following year. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Founders Heritage Park, Nelson
pois.short: A living heritage village — 30+ period buildings with working blacksmith, printer, and operating 610mm heritage railway.
pois.commentary: Founders Heritage Park brings together relocated and replica buildings from the 1840s–1940s. 610mm tourist railway operates on fine-weather weekends.
pois.audio_story: You are at Founders Heritage Park, an open air museum on the inlet behind the port of Nelson. The park is arranged as a small heritage village, and over thirty buildings representing Nelson life from the eighteen forties to the nineteen forties have been either relocated here or reconstructed in period form. A colonial hospital, a schoolroom, a general store with period groceries on the shelves, a wool store, a printer''s shop with working letterpress machines, a tannery, a blacksmith, and a jeweller, among others. On fine weekends a working six hundred and ten millimetre narrow gauge tourist railway runs through the village, pulled by a small steam or diesel locomotive depending on the day. A craft brewery, a café, and a gallery of period curiosities round out the visit. Entry is modest. Allow two to three hours for a relaxed walk through. The park is run by a local charitable trust and volunteers, and is understandably local in flavour, which is part of its charm. It is not a professionally polished tourist experience. It is the slow, honest, craft heavy work of a community that has taken responsibility for preserving its own memory. A good one to pair with the Nelson Provincial Museum in town.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Founders Heritage Park, Nelson — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A living heritage village — 30+ period buildings with working blacksmith, printer, and operating 610mm heritage railway. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Te Āwhina Marae, Motueka
pois.short: The principal marae of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in Motueka — viewed respectfully from the road.
pois.commentary: Te Āwhina Marae at Motueka serves Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua descendants who migrated south from Taranaki in the 1820s. Wharenui Tūrangapeke.
pois.audio_story: You are passing Te Āwhina Marae, the principal marae at Motueka, and the home of the descendants of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in the Tasman district. Both iwi migrated south from Taranaki in the eighteen twenties under pressure from the northern Musket Wars, pushed down the west coast of the North Island, across Cook Strait, and into the top of the South Island, where they settled alongside and in some cases displaced the earlier tangata whenua. The wharenui at Te Āwhina is called Tūrangapeke, named for a senior ancestor of the iwi. It stands on the flat behind Motueka, within a stone''s throw of the beach. The marae has been an important centre for the southern Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua communities for over a century, hosting tangihanga, weddings, and major cultural and political hui. It is a living working marae. Casual visitors are not admitted, and the site should be viewed respectfully from the road. Pause for a moment. Remember that the iwi of Motueka are not tangata whenua from the time of first arrival but are themselves migrants from Taranaki whose migration and settlement here is barely two hundred years old, still fresh in their own traditions.
sources_to_check: Te Āti Awa o te Waka a Māui

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Āwhina Marae, Motueka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The principal marae of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in Motueka — viewed respectfully from the road. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Te Waikoropupū Springs
pois.short: The largest and clearest freshwater springs in New Zealand — wāhi tapu to local iwi, protected from all physical contact.
pois.commentary: Te Waikoropupū releases 14,000 l/s of crystalline water. Water clarity measured over 60m. Wāhi tapu under a Water Conservation Order; no contact permitted.
pois.audio_story: You are at Te Waikoropupū, known in English as Pupu Springs, which emerge from the limestone bedrock of Golden Bay and discharge around fourteen thousand litres of water per second into a complex of clear pools and channels. The water is filtered through the karst system for decades before it surfaces here, and the visual clarity, measured by scientific divers, regularly exceeds sixty metres, among the clearest fresh water ever recorded anywhere in the world. Te Waikoropupū is wāhi tapu to the iwi of Mohua, particularly Ngāti Tama and Te Āti Awa, and to local Pākehā residents who have campaigned for its protection for generations. Swimming, diving, drinking, or even touching the water is not permitted, both as a practical measure to protect its exceptional clarity from skin oils and sediment, and as respect for its spiritual status. Views from the boardwalk are more than adequate. A Water Conservation Order, one of only a handful in the country, formally recognises the springs'' cultural and scientific value. Walk the boardwalk slowly. Do not cross the rope. Stand and watch the sand dance in the upwelling for as long as you can. In a country full of freshwater wonders, this is arguably the most precious.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and clearest freshwater springs in New Zealand — wāhi tapu to local iwi, protected from all physical contact. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Farewell Spit / Onetahua
pois.short: The 26km curving sand spit at the top of the South Island — Cape Farewell, where Cook finally turned home in 1770.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit is 26km long. Named by Cook in March 1770 as his last point of contact with NZ before sailing home. Onetahua is the Māori name.
pois.audio_story: You are at Farewell Spit, the long curving sand spit that arcs out twenty six kilometres from the northern tip of the South Island, enclosing Golden Bay. In Māori tradition the spit is called Onetahua, the piled up sand, and is regarded as a tupuna, ancestral form, by Ngāti Tama and the iwi of Mohua. The English name comes from Captain James Cook, who on the thirty first of March, seventeen seventy, sailed past the point as his final sight of land before striking out west across the Tasman Sea for Australia. He named the cape Farewell. The spit functions as the principal feeding ground for tens of thousands of migratory shorebirds each summer, including bar tailed godwits, knots, and oystercatchers, and is recognised as an internationally important wetland. It is also notorious as one of the great whale stranding sites in the world. Several times each decade, large pods of pilot whales strand on the flat sand of the inner spit, probably confused by the shallow bathymetry. Local and DOC response operations to refloat them are a major feature of West Coast and Tasman public life. Public foot access is permitted for about four kilometres from the base; the tip itself, including the lighthouse, can only be reached by commercial guided tour.

reviews.summary: Farewell Spit / Onetahua draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 26km curving sand spit at the top of the South Island — Cape Farewell, where Cook finally turned home in 1770. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Collingwood Old Cemetery
pois.short: A quiet hillside cemetery above Collingwood — the burial ground of the 1857 Aorere gold rush miners.
pois.commentary: The Aorere gold rush began 1857 near Collingwood. The town grew rapidly to 2,000 people before the field played out. Old cemetery overlooks the town.
pois.audio_story: You are at the old cemetery above Collingwood, at the northern end of Golden Bay. Collingwood as a town is a direct legacy of the Aorere gold rush of eighteen fifty seven, the first significant gold rush in New Zealand. Before eighteen fifty seven there was nothing here but a small surveyor''s camp. Within six months of the first strike in the upper Aorere River there were over two thousand men camped in and around the settlement, working sluice boxes in the creeks and living in rough tent and wooden shanty camps. The town was properly named Collingwood in eighteen fifty nine, supposedly at one point being considered as a possible new capital of New Zealand. By eighteen sixty two the easy surface gold had been worked out and the rush moved on to the Otago fields. Collingwood, its population collapsed back to a few hundred, reverted to a quiet port serving farming and logging communities. The old cemetery above the town holds the graves of many of those who stayed and died here. Weather beaten headstones, Chinese inscriptions on a few plots, young children''s graves. A quiet thirty minutes. Look down into the estuary as you walk out, and imagine the tent city it once was.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Collingwood Old Cemetery — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A quiet hillside cemetery above Collingwood — the burial ground of the 1857 Aorere gold rush miners. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Abel Tasman''s First Encounter Site — Wainui Bay
pois.short: Where the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman first met Māori in 1642 — a meeting that ended in four Dutch deaths.
pois.commentary: In December 1642 Abel Tasman anchored off Wainui Bay. Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri attacked a launch; four Dutch sailors were killed. Tasman left without landing.
pois.audio_story: You are at Wainui Bay, near the inner edge of Golden Bay, and on the thirteenth and fourteenth of December, sixteen forty two, Dutch ships anchored in the open water you are looking at. Abel Tasman, sailing for the Dutch East India Company on a voyage of exploration, had already sighted the West Coast of the South Island. He was looking for an anchorage, fresh water, and possible trade. A number of waka came out from the shore here. The parties on either side exchanged calls. Trumpets were blown and drums beaten from both vessels, which Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri, the iwi of Mohua at the time, appear to have interpreted as a direct challenge. The following morning, as the Dutch launched a small boat between the two ships, the tangata whenua attacked. Four Dutch sailors were killed. Tasman weighed anchor without landing and left the country forever, naming the bay Murderers Bay, a name that clung on colonial maps for two hundred years. He never set foot on New Zealand soil. The first and second encounters between Europeans and Māori were separated by more than a century. Drive to the shore, stand in the quiet bay, and imagine what that misunderstanding must have looked like.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Abel Tasman''s First Encounter Site — Wainui Bay — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman first met Māori in 1642 — a meeting that ended in four Dutch deaths. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: The Suter Art Gallery, Nelson
pois.short: The oldest art gallery in the country still on its original site — and a wonderful collection of early NZ painting.
pois.commentary: The Suter opened 1899. Named after Bishop Andrew Suter. Rebuilt 2016. Strong collection of early NZ painters including John Gully and Olivia Spencer Bower.
pois.audio_story: You are at the Suter Art Gallery, on the edge of Queens Gardens in central Nelson. It opened in eighteen ninety nine under a bequest from Bishop Andrew Suter, the second Anglican bishop of Nelson, who had amassed a significant personal collection of paintings and wanted them gifted to his adopted city. The original Edwardian building was extended several times over the twentieth century and then, in twenty sixteen, was completely rebuilt behind the original facade in a bold modern extension that triples the exhibition space. The Suter''s permanent collection is strong on early New Zealand watercolours and oils, particularly the nineteenth century Nelson painter John Gully, whose landscapes of the Nelson Lakes and the Buller Gorge remain among the best of their era. The gallery also holds important works by Olivia Spencer Bower, Rita Angus, Sir Mountford Tosswill Woollaston, and Colin McCahon, many with direct connections to the Nelson and Tasman art communities of the twentieth century. Temporary exhibitions rotate monthly and typically include contemporary local artists. Entry is free for most of the permanent galleries. An hour well spent, and the café is one of the best in the central city.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at The Suter Art Gallery, Nelson is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The oldest art gallery in the country still on its original site — and a wonderful collection of early NZ painting. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Te Waikoropupū Springs — Sacred Waters
pois.short: NZ's largest freshwater springs — wāhi tapu with some of the clearest water on Earth.
pois.commentary: Visibility exceeds 60 metres. The springs were on the pounamu trade route. Visitors are asked not to enter the water. The springs are the tears of Papatūānuku.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs — Sacred Waters as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. NZ's largest freshwater springs — wāhi tapu with some of the clearest water on Earth. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Rangi & Papa Sculpture, Marahau
pois.short: A carved sculpture of sky father and earth mother at the entrance to Abel Tasman National Park.
pois.commentary: The sculpture is a reminder that these landscapes carry deep Māori meaning beyond their natural beauty. The park was home to Māori for centuries before Abel Tasman's arrival in 1642.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Rangi & Papa Sculpture, Marahau as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A carved sculpture of sky father and earth mother at the entrance to Abel Tasman National Park. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Farewell Spit / Onetahua
pois.short: The spirits' departure point — where the dead travel before leaping to the underworld.
pois.commentary: In some traditions, spirits travel northward along the spit before leaping to Te Rerenga Wairua. The 26 km spit is wāhi tapu and home to vast numbers of migratory birds.

reviews.summary: Farewell Spit / Onetahua draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The spirits' departure point — where the dead travel before leaping to the underworld. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Whakatū — Nelson Māori Heritage
pois.short: Whakatū (Nelson) was settled by Māori centuries before Europeans — rich in pā sites and cultural associations.
pois.commentary: Whakatū has a complex Māori history involving multiple iwi — Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri, Ngāti Kuia, Rangitāne, Ngāti Toa, and Te Ātiawa all have associations with the area. The Founders Heritage Park and Isel House contain Māori artefacts.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Whakatū — Nelson Māori Heritage as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Whakatū (Nelson) was settled by Māori centuries before Europeans — rich in pā sites and cultural associations. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Coast Track
pois.short: New Zealand's most popular Great Walk — 60 km of golden sand beaches and turquoise bays.
pois.commentary: The Abel Tasman Coast Track covers 60 km from Marahau to Wainui, typically walked in 3-5 days. It passes through native bush and along a succession of golden sand beaches with crystal-clear water. It is the most accessible Great Walk and can also be combined with sea kayaking. The park was established in 1942 on the 300th anniversary of Abel Tasman's visit.
pois.audio_story: You are stepping onto the Abel Tasman Coast Track at Marahau, and for the next 60 kilometres, golden beaches will keep appearing around every headland like gifts. This is New Zealand's smallest national park but arguably its most beautiful, a scalloped coastline of granite and golden sand backed by native bush. The track is gentle by New Zealand standards — no alpine crossings, no exposed ridges — which is why it is the most walked Great Walk in the country. You will wade across tidal estuaries, watch seals basking on Tonga Island, and swim in water so clear you can count the fish. The park was created in 1942 to mark the 300th anniversary of Dutch navigator Abel Tasman's arrival, though Tasman himself never actually landed — Maori in waka drove him off after a violent encounter in Golden Bay.

reviews.summary: The Abel Tasman Coast Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. New Zealand's most popular Great Walk — 60 km of golden sand beaches and turquoise bays. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Heaphy Track Great Walk
pois.short: The longest Great Walk crosses Kahurangi National Park from alpine downs to wild West Coast beaches.
pois.commentary: The Heaphy Track is 78.4 km long, running from Brown Hut near Collingwood to Kohaihai on the West Coast, typically walked in 4-6 days. It crosses open tussock downs, dense podocarp forest, and a dramatic stretch of nikau palm-lined coast. Since 2011, mountain biking has been permitted seasonally. It is named after Major Charles Heaphy, who explored the route in 1846.
pois.audio_story: You are beginning the Heaphy Track, and over the next 78 kilometres you will cross an entire national park from east to west. The track starts in beech forest, climbs to the Gouland Downs — an eerie plateau of tussock and limestone — and then descends through layer after layer of forest into the West Coast. The final coastal section is extraordinary: you walk along wild beaches backed by nikau palms, the bush tumbling down steep hillsides to the Tasman Sea. Major Charles Heaphy explored this route in 1846, though Maori had used it for centuries before to access West Coast pounamu. Since 2011, mountain bikers have been allowed on the track between May and September, making it the only Great Walk that permits cycling.

reviews.summary: The Heaphy Track Great Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. The longest Great Walk crosses Kahurangi National Park from alpine downs to wild West Coast beaches. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Great Taste Trail Cycle Ride
pois.short: Cycle 174 km through the Nelson-Tasman region's wineries, breweries, and coastal villages.
pois.commentary: The Great Taste Trail is a 174 km network of cycling trails linking Nelson, Richmond, Mapua, Motueka, and Kaiteriteri. The trail passes through hop gardens, vineyards, craft breweries, and along the coast. The region produces 90% of New Zealand's hops and has a thriving craft beer and artisan food scene.
pois.audio_story: You are pedalling the Great Taste Trail, and the name is not an accident — this ride is designed to take you past the best food and drink the Nelson-Tasman region produces. The trail follows old railway lines and coastal paths through 174 kilometres of some of New Zealand's sunniest countryside. You will cycle past hop gardens that supply 90 percent of the country's hops, through vineyards producing aromatic whites, and past craft breweries that seem to appear every time you need a break. At Mapua, the old wharf has been converted into a cluster of cafes and galleries, and the estuary crossing by barge is a highlight. Between tastings, the trail threads along the Tasman Bay coast, through apple orchards, and into the foothills of Kahurangi National Park.

reviews.summary: The Great Taste Trail Cycle Ride route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle 174 km through the Nelson-Tasman region's wineries, breweries, and coastal villages. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Takaka Hill Drive (SH60)
pois.short: Climb the marble mountain road that is Golden Bay's only road connection to the outside world.
pois.commentary: Takaka Hill on SH60 rises to 791 m and is the sole road link between Motueka and Golden Bay. The road winds through marble and limestone karst country with caves, sinkholes, and natural arches. Ngarua Caves near the summit offer guided tours through spectacular formations. The hill was long considered one of New Zealand's most challenging drives.
pois.audio_story: You are climbing Takaka Hill, the marble mountain that separates Golden Bay from the rest of New Zealand. This is the only road in, and it earns the effort. SH60 switchbacks up through a landscape of limestone and marble karst — you will see sinkholes in paddocks, cave entrances in road cuttings, and if you stop at Ngarua Caves near the summit, you can walk through caverns of pure marble draped in stalactites. The geology here is extraordinary: this marble formed from ancient sea-floor sediments hundreds of millions of years ago, then was lifted, folded, and dissolved into the honeycomb landscape you see today. At the top, pull over at the lookout. On a clear day, you can see the entire sweep of Golden Bay, with Farewell Spit curving out into the Tasman Sea like a beckoning finger.

reviews.summary: The Takaka Hill Drive (SH60) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Climb the marble mountain road that is Golden Bay's only road connection to the outside world. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Farewell Spit Heritage Walk
pois.short: Walk the base of New Zealand's longest sand spit — a 26 km bird sanctuary curving into the Tasman Sea.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit is a 26 km sand spit extending from the northern tip of the South Island, formed by longshore drift carrying sand from the West Coast. It is a wetland of international importance under the Ramsar Convention and home to over 90 bird species including migratory godwits from Alaska. Public access beyond the base requires a licensed tour operator.
pois.audio_story: You are walking the base of Farewell Spit, and ahead of you 26 kilometres of sand curves out into the Tasman Sea like a great golden hook. This is New Zealand's longest natural sand spit, built grain by grain from West Coast sand carried north by currents over thousands of years. The spit is a bird sanctuary of global significance — each year, bar-tailed godwits arrive here after an 11,000-kilometre non-stop flight from Alaska, the longest known migration of any bird. You can only explore the base on foot without a guide; the outer reaches are strictly protected. But even from here, the sight is remarkable: vast tidal flats stretching to the horizon, alive with wading birds, and the lighthouse blinking at the very tip where the spit finally surrenders to the sea.

reviews.summary: The 26km sand spit curving into the top of the South Island is a remarkable natural feature — home to internationally significant shorebird habitat and gannet colonies. Visitors can only access the full spit on guided tours, which reviewers consistently rate as excellent value for the unique experience.

pois.name: Wangapeka Track
pois.short: A rugged 52 km backcountry track through Kahurangi's remote interior — the tramper's alternative to the Heaphy.
pois.commentary: The Wangapeka Track runs 52 km between Rolling River near Tapawera and Little Wanganui on the West Coast, crossing the main divide of the Kahurangi Range. It is considered more challenging than the Heaphy Track, with steeper terrain and river crossings. The track passes through diverse forest types and crosses two alpine saddles above 1,000 m.
pois.audio_story: You are setting out on the Wangapeka Track, and this is the road less travelled through Kahurangi National Park. While most people walk the Heaphy, the Wangapeka offers something rawer: 52 kilometres of river valleys, alpine saddles, and forest so thick it blocks out the sky. The track crosses two passes above 1,000 metres and involves genuine river crossings where you will get your boots wet. It takes 4-5 days and you will likely have the huts to yourself — a rare luxury in a country where the popular tracks can feel crowded. The forest changes constantly as you cross the divide: beech gives way to podocarp, rata and rimu tower overhead, and if you are lucky, you might hear the booming call of a great spotted kiwi echoing through the night.

reviews.summary: The Wangapeka Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A rugged 52 km backcountry track through Kahurangi's remote interior — the tramper's alternative to the Heaphy. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Cable Bay Walkway
pois.short: Walk the coastal ridge where the first telegraph cable linked New Zealand's two islands in 1866.
pois.commentary: The Cable Bay Walkway follows the coast from Glenduan to Cable Bay, covering 8 km one way. The route traces the ridgeline above the boulder-strewn coast with panoramic views of Tasman Bay and the Marlborough Sounds. Cable Bay takes its name from the undersea telegraph cable laid here in 1866, connecting the North and South Islands for the first time.
pois.audio_story: You are walking the Cable Bay Walkway, following a ridgeline above the northern coast of Nelson. The views are extraordinary — Tasman Bay stretches south, the Marlborough Sounds crumple the horizon to the east, and on clear days you can see the North Island across Cook Strait. The bay at the far end takes its name from a moment in New Zealand's history: in 1866, an undersea telegraph cable was laid here, connecting the South Island to Wellington for the first time. Before that cable, news took days to cross the strait by ship. After it, messages flew in seconds. The walkway itself undulates along the coast through regenerating bush and open grassland, with the constant blue of the bay below.

reviews.summary: The Cable Bay Walkway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk the coastal ridge where the first telegraph cable linked New Zealand's two islands in 1866. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Motueka Valley Highway
pois.short: Drive through the hop gardens and orchards of the Motueka River valley to the mountains beyond.
pois.commentary: The Motueka Valley Highway follows the Motueka River inland from the coast through some of New Zealand's most productive horticultural land. The valley produces hops, apples, kiwifruit, and berries. The road continues to the Upper Motueka and the eastern boundary of Kahurangi National Park, offering mountain views throughout.
pois.audio_story: You are driving up the Motueka Valley, and the roadside is a patchwork of orchards and hop gardens. Those tall wire frames draped in green vines are hop plants, and this valley produces most of New Zealand's hops — the flowers that give beer its bitterness and aroma. The craft beer revolution has been very good to this valley. Apple orchards line the river flats too, their neat rows blooming white in spring and heavy with fruit in autumn. The Motueka River itself is a renowned trout stream, its clear water winding through willows and gravel banks. As you drive deeper into the valley, the mountains close in — the western flanks of Kahurangi National Park rising steeply from the valley floor.

reviews.summary: The Motueka Valley Highway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through the hop gardens and orchards of the Motueka River valley to the mountains beyond. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri to Marahau Coastal Road
pois.short: A short coastal drive between two beloved beach towns at the gateway to Abel Tasman.
pois.commentary: The short drive between Kaiteriteri and Marahau passes through native bush along the coast at the southern entrance to Abel Tasman National Park. Kaiteriteri is famous for its orange-gold granite sand beach, while Marahau is the main trailhead for the Abel Tasman Coast Track. Both offer water taxi and kayak services into the park.
pois.audio_story: You are driving the coastal road from Kaiteriteri to Marahau, and these few kilometres are the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park. Kaiteriteri's beach behind you is famous for its orange-gold sand — the colour comes from the granite rock of this coast, rich in iron and feldspar. The road winds through native bush with glimpses of the turquoise Tasman Bay through the trees. At Marahau, you reach the start of the Abel Tasman Coast Track, where water taxis idle at the wharf and kayakers prepare to paddle into some of the most beautiful coastal scenery in New Zealand. Even if you are not walking the full track, this is where the adventure begins.

reviews.summary: The Kaiteriteri to Marahau Coastal Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A short coastal drive between two beloved beach towns at the gateway to Abel Tasman. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Wharariki Beach Walk
pois.short: Walk to the wild, wind-sculpted Wharariki Beach with its massive archway islands and fur seal colony.
pois.commentary: Wharariki Beach is an exposed, wind-swept beach near Cape Farewell, the northernmost point of the South Island. The 20-minute walk from the car park crosses farmland and sand dunes to reveal dramatic archway islands, rock pools, and a fur seal colony. The beach is famous for its windswept sand formations and is one of the most photographed beaches in New Zealand.
pois.audio_story: You are walking across farmland toward Wharariki Beach, and nothing prepares you for what appears over the sand dunes. The beach is enormous, wild, and dominated by massive archway islands — rock stacks the size of buildings with holes punched clean through by the sea. The wind here comes straight off the Tasman Sea with nothing to slow it, sculpting the sand into rippled patterns that change by the hour. At the western end, a colony of New Zealand fur seals hauls out on the rocks, their pups playing in the rock pools with an energy that is hard not to smile at. This is Cape Farewell country — the northernmost point of the South Island — and the name is apt. This feels like the edge of things, where the land surrenders to the ocean and the ocean wins.

reviews.summary: The Wharariki Beach Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to the wild, wind-sculpted Wharariki Beach with its massive archway islands and fur seal colony. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking
pois.short: Sea kayak along golden beaches and turquoise coves in Abel Tasman National Park.
pois.commentary: Abel Tasman National Park offers some of New Zealand's finest sea kayaking, with golden sand beaches, clear turquoise water, and native bush reaching to the water's edge. Multi-day guided and freedom kayak trips explore the coast, with stops at pristine beaches and chances to see seals, dolphins, and blue penguins.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Abel Tasman National Park, New Zealand's smallest national park but arguably its most beautiful coastal playground. Your sea kayak will carry you along a coastline of golden sand beaches separated by granite headlands, with water so clear you can see the starfish on the seabed three metres below your paddle. The park stretches 51 kilometres along the coast, and by kayak you'll access beaches that are impossible to reach any other way. Paddle into the famous Split Apple Rock — a giant boulder split perfectly in two by geological forces — and stop for lunch on a deserted beach where the sand squeaks beneath your feet. Fur seals laze on offshore rocks, little blue penguins pop up beside your kayak, and if you're here in summer, the water temperature is warm enough for swimming. This is sea kayaking at its absolute finest, and there's nowhere else quite like it in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sea kayak along golden beaches and turquoise coves in Abel Tasman National Park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Skydive Abel Tasman
pois.short: Tandem skydive with views of Abel Tasman's golden coast and Tasman Bay.
pois.commentary: Skydive Abel Tasman offers tandem jumps from up to 16,500 feet over one of New Zealand's most scenic coastlines. Freefall views encompass Abel Tasman National Park, Tasman Bay, the Richmond Range, and on clear days, both the North and South Islands.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the drop zone for Skydive Abel Tasman, where you're about to freefall over one of the most beautiful coastlines in New Zealand. The plane will climb to 16,500 feet, and as it does, the golden beaches and turquoise bays of Abel Tasman National Park will unfurl below you like a postcard come to life. When you jump, the freefall lasts up to 75 seconds — long enough to take in views that span Tasman Bay, the Richmond Range, and on a clear day, the North Island across Cook Strait. The parachute ride down is sublime, floating above that famous golden coastline with the bush-clad hills and blue water spread out beneath you. Landing on Kaiteriteri Beach — one of New Zealand's most popular holiday beaches — is the perfect finale to an extraordinary jump.

reviews.summary: The Skydive Abel Tasman adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem skydive with views of Abel Tasman's golden coast and Tasman Bay. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Canyoning
pois.short: Abseil waterfalls and leap into pools in Abel Tasman's hidden granite canyons.
pois.commentary: Canyoning in the Abel Tasman region takes you into hidden granite gorges with waterfall abseils, natural rock slides, and deep plunge pools. The canyon environments are lush with native bush and feature the distinctive golden granite of the region. Guided trips cater to beginners and experienced adventurers.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the start of a canyoning adventure that will take you into the hidden heart of the Abel Tasman hinterland. While most visitors stick to the famous coastline, the hills behind the national park conceal dramatic granite canyons carved by millennia of rainfall. You'll abseil down waterfalls that cascade over golden granite, slide through natural rock chutes polished to glass by centuries of water, and leap into deep plunge pools surrounded by lush native bush. The Abel Tasman's distinctive golden granite gives these canyons a warmth and beauty that's unique in New Zealand. Your guides will teach you the rope skills and safety techniques as you go, so no prior experience is necessary. By the end of the day, you'll have discovered a side of Abel Tasman that most people never even know exists.

reviews.summary: The Abel Tasman Canyoning adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Abseil waterfalls and leap into pools in Abel Tasman's hidden granite canyons. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Cable Bay Stand-Up Paddleboarding
pois.short: SUP the calm turquoise waters of Cable Bay near Nelson.
pois.commentary: Cable Bay, just north of Nelson, offers sheltered waters ideal for stand-up paddleboarding. The small bay has a sandy beach, clear water, and views across Tasman Bay to the Richmond Range. Rental and lessons are available locally, and the bay is sheltered from the prevailing winds.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Cable Bay, a sheltered crescent of golden sand just north of Nelson that offers perfect conditions for stand-up paddleboarding. The bay is naturally protected from the prevailing winds, creating calm, clear water that's ideal for beginners and experienced paddlers alike. From your board, you'll glide over a seabed of sand and rock, watching fish dart beneath you in the crystal-clear water. The views across Tasman Bay to the snow-dusted Richmond Range are stunning, and the gentle shoreline makes it easy to hop off for a swim whenever the mood strikes. Nelson is one of the sunniest cities in New Zealand, so chances are you'll have blue sky and warm sunshine to complement your paddle. It's a relaxing, beautiful way to spend a morning or afternoon on the water.

reviews.summary: The Cable Bay Stand-Up Paddleboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. SUP the calm turquoise waters of Cable Bay near Nelson. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Nelson Mountain Bike Trails
pois.short: Ride Nelson's legendary mountain bike trails, some of NZ's best singletrack.
pois.commentary: Nelson is renowned as one of New Zealand's top mountain biking destinations, with trail networks at Codgers, Fringed Hill, and the Coppermine Trail. The trails benefit from Nelson's dry, sunny climate and range from flowing beginner tracks to technical expert singletrack. The Great Taste Trail offers scenic road and rail-trail cycling.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Nelson, a city that punches well above its weight when it comes to mountain biking. The trail networks here are legendary in New Zealand cycling circles — Codgers offers tight, technical singletrack through native bush; Fringed Hill delivers flowing descents with harbour views; and the Coppermine Trail is a purpose-built masterpiece of berms, jumps, and drops. What gives Nelson its edge is the climate — it's the sunniest city in New Zealand, which means the trails dry quickly and ride well for most of the year. The local riding community is passionate and has built an incredible network of tracks that would rival any mountain biking destination in the world. If you prefer something gentler, the Great Taste Trail offers 174 kilometres of scenic cycling through vineyards, orchards, and along the coast. Nelson is a cycling city through and through.

reviews.summary: The Nelson Mountain Bike Trails adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride Nelson's legendary mountain bike trails, some of NZ's best singletrack. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Farewell Spit Eco Tour
pois.short: 4WD safari along the extraordinary 35 km Farewell Spit — a globally significant bird habitat.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit is a 35-kilometre sand spit at the northern tip of the South Island, designated a Wetland of International Importance. Guided 4WD tours are the only way to access the spit beyond the public walking area. Over 90 bird species use the spit, including thousands of migratory godwits from Alaska.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Farewell Spit, a remarkable 35-kilometre finger of sand that curls into Golden Bay at the very top of the South Island. This is one of the most important bird habitats in the Southern Hemisphere, home to over 90 species and a critical stopover for bar-tailed godwits on their epic 11,000-kilometre non-stop flight from Alaska — the longest known non-stop migration of any bird. A guided 4WD tour is the only way to explore the spit beyond the first four kilometres, and your vehicle will travel along the ocean beach past the lighthouse to viewing points where thousands of wading birds feed on the vast tidal flats. The scale of the spit is astonishing — the sand dunes rise to 30 metres in places, and on a windy day, the sand streams from the crests like smoke. The Maori name, Onetahua, means 'heaped-up sand,' and it's been accumulating here for over 6,000 years.

reviews.summary: The Farewell Spit Eco Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. 4WD safari along the extraordinary 35 km Farewell Spit — a globally significant bird habitat. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Golden Bay Horse Trekking
pois.short: Ride horses along the wild beaches and through bush in Golden Bay.
pois.commentary: Horse trekking in Golden Bay offers rides along wide sandy beaches, through native bush, and across farmland with views of Farewell Spit. The area's remote, uncrowded character makes for a peaceful riding experience. Rides cater to all experience levels.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Golden Bay, one of the most peaceful and unspoiled corners of the South Island, and a wonderful place to explore on horseback. Your ride will take you along wide golden sand beaches where the only hoofprints ahead of you are from previous treks, through pockets of native bush alive with birdsong, and across farmland with views stretching to Farewell Spit and the distant peaks of Kahurangi National Park. Golden Bay has a relaxed, creative community and a landscape that seems to slow time down. The horses are well-suited to the terrain, picking their way confidently along beach and bush tracks. Whether you're an experienced rider or sitting in a saddle for the first time, the combination of gentle horses, stunning scenery, and Golden Bay's special atmosphere makes this a ride you'll remember long after you leave.

reviews.summary: The Golden Bay Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses along the wild beaches and through bush in Golden Bay. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri Snorkelling & Marine Reserve
pois.short: Snorkel the Tonga Island Marine Reserve in Abel Tasman's crystal-clear waters.
pois.commentary: The Tonga Island Marine Reserve within Abel Tasman National Park protects a rich marine environment around Tonga Island and its surrounds. Snorkelling reveals colourful reef fish, anemones, and kelp forests in clear water. Accessible by water taxi from Kaiteriteri, with free access once in the park.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Kaiteriteri, the golden gateway to Abel Tasman National Park and some of the best snorkelling on the South Island. A short water taxi ride takes you to the Tonga Island Marine Reserve, where the water clarity is extraordinary — you can see the seabed clearly at depths of ten metres or more. Slip beneath the surface and you'll discover a world of colourful reef fish darting through swaying kelp forests, anemones waving in the gentle current, and if you keep very still, you might spot a blue cod or octopus hiding in the rocky crevices. The marine reserve has been protected since 1993, and the abundance of life here shows what healthy seas can look like. Tonga Island itself is home to a colony of New Zealand fur seals, and they sometimes swim curiously close to snorkellers. The water is warmest from December to March, but a wetsuit extends the season comfortably.

reviews.summary: The Kaiteriteri Snorkelling & Marine Reserve adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Snorkel the Tonga Island Marine Reserve in Abel Tasman's crystal-clear waters. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Golden Bay Paragliding
pois.short: Tandem paraglide over Golden Bay's sweeping beaches and mountains.
pois.commentary: Tandem paragliding from the hills above Takaka offers bird's-eye views of Golden Bay, Farewell Spit, and the Kahurangi ranges. Flights launch from hill sites with reliable thermals and land on the beach or open farmland. The coastal setting provides stunning contrasts of sea, sand, and mountain.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the top of the South Island's most spectacular paragliding launch sites. From the hills above Takaka, you and your tandem pilot will run a few steps into the air and soar out over Golden Bay with a view that will make your heart sing. Below you, the sweep of the bay curves away to the sand ribbon of Farewell Spit. Behind you, the peaks of Kahurangi National Park march into the distance. The thermals along this coast are reliable and gentle, often providing long, smooth flights that let you truly absorb the panoramic scenery. Your landing will be on the beach or a green field, and the experience of stepping out of the sky onto solid ground again is always a gentle surprise. Golden Bay's remote beauty is best appreciated from the air, where the full scale of the landscape reveals itself.

reviews.summary: The Golden Bay Paragliding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem paraglide over Golden Bay's sweeping beaches and mountains. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.
